content: It must be earnings day, because Tesla is making a Robotaxi announcement. The company said in a post on X that it was launching its Robotaxi service in Orlando and Tampa, attaching a pair of maps that show each city’s service area. No word on fleet size or whether interested customers would be able to hail a vehicle right away. The timing of the post is noteworthy, considering later today Tesla CEO Elon Musk is expected to provide an update on the progress of the company’s Robotaxi service during its second quarter earnings call. Adding two new cities to the docket probably sounds like progress to some investors. [Media: https://twitter.com/robotaxi/status/2079551968725303500] Of course, that progress is entirely subjective. The company’s all-important Robotaxi service is dramatically smaller than Elon Musk predicted. In 2024, he said the company’s autonomous ridehail vehicles would be available to half the US population by the end of 2025. Several months later, Musk said Tesla would have “500 or more” vehicles in the Austin area alone by the end of 2025. The number of vehicles in each city has also fluctuated dramatically: back in May, Tesla had five unsupervised robotaxis in Dallas, six in Houston, and 29 in Austin. As of today, there are only 17 unsupervised vehicles in Austin, four in Dallas, and zero in Houston, according to the Robotaxi Tracker. By comparison, Waymo is estimated to have over 3,500 fully driverless vehicles in operation across over 10 cities. Like Waymo, Tesla typically introduces robotaxis to a new city with a safety driver behind the wheel. Unlike Waymo, sometimes Tesla moves that person over to the passenger seat with access to a kill switch should anything go wrong. The company has been inconsistent in how it rolls out its robotaxis to new markets. The numbers go up and down, with zero explanation from the company as to why. According to several reports, wait times for Tesla’s robotaxis can be long as a result of limited vehicle supply. Often the vehicles pick up and drop off in the wrong location. Unsupervised vehicles are often remotely operated. This week, Tesla reported that an unsupervised robotaxi crashed into a tree stump while being remotely operated. Musk has predicted the service will be profitable in 2027. By adding new cities, Tesla is clearly trying to sell investors on the idea that its robotaxi project is growing. But the fluctuating fleet size, inconsistency in supervised vs unsupervised vehicles, and long wait times tell a very different story.
chineseTitle: 特斯拉机器人出租车前往佛罗里达州
chineseSummary: 今天肯定是财报日，因为特斯拉正在宣布推出 Robotaxi。该公司在 X 上的一篇帖子中表示，将在奥兰多和坦帕推出 Robotaxi 服务，并附上两张显示每个城市服务区域的地图。没有关于机队规模或感兴趣的客户是否能够[...]的消息
chineseContent: 今天肯定是财报日，因为特斯拉正在宣布推出 Robotaxi。该公司在 X 上的一篇帖子中表示，它将在奥兰多和坦帕推出 Robotaxi 服务，并附上两张显示每个城市服务区域的地图。没有关于车队规模或感兴趣的客户是否能够立即叫车的消息。考虑到今天晚些时候，特斯拉首席执行官埃隆·马斯克预计将在第二季度财报电话会议上提供该公司 Robotaxi 服务进展的最新信息，因此这篇文章的发布时机值得注意。对于一些投资者来说，将两个新城市添加到待办事项中可能听起来像是进步。 [媒体：https://twitter.com/robotaxi/status/2079551968725303500]当然，这种进步完全是主观的。该公司最重要的 Robotaxi 服务比埃隆·马斯克 (Elon Musk) 的预测要小得多。 2024 年，他表示，到 2025 年底，该公司的自动驾驶出租车将覆盖美国一半人口。几个月后，马斯克表示，到 2025 年底，特斯拉仅在奥斯汀地区就将拥有“500 辆或更多”车辆。每个城市的车辆数量也出现了巨大波动：早在 5 月份，特斯拉在达拉斯就有 5 辆无人监管的机器人出租车，在休斯顿有 6 辆，在奥斯汀有 29 辆。根据 Robotaxi Tracker 的数据，截至目前，奥斯汀只有 17 辆无人监管车辆，达拉斯有 4 辆，休斯顿有 0 辆。相比之下，Waymo 预计在 10 多个城市运营超过 3,500 辆完全无人驾驶汽车。与 Waymo 一样，特斯拉通常会将机器人出租车引入新城市，并由安全驾驶员负责驾驶。与 Waymo 不同的是，如果出现任何问题，特斯拉有时会将该人转移到乘客座位上，以便使用终止开关。该公司在将机器人出租车推向新高度的方式上一直不一致
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于 AI 与科技趋势信号，适合用于观察工具选型、产品机会、开发者关注点和技术路线变化。
